one_line_takeaway: A handful of medical QA samples carrying fluent-but-false rationales can stealthily corrupt a compact medical LLM's reasoning on a targeted subject during LoRA SFT -- but only within a narrow poison count-and-ratio window and only when no correct target-subject samples are present.
key_finding: Rationale poisoning works where knowledge overwriting does not: 125 poisoned samples (8.8% ratio) with 1,300 non-fever correct samples dropped Qwen3-4B-Base fever accuracy by 8.2% (0.798 to 0.716) while non-fever accuracy fell only 3.2%, whereas all four knowledge-overwriting variants left fever accuracy essentially unchanged (0.798-0.820). The attack requires BOTH a minimum count and a minimum ratio -- 125 poison + 2,600 correct (lower ratio) failed (fever 0.787), and only after doubling poison to 250 did it succeed (0.727); pushing poison too high (500+1,300) further lowered fever acc to 0.661 but crashed non-fever acc to 0.711, breaking stealth. Correct target-subject samples neutralize the attack (fever acc climbs back toward baseline as 0-400 correct fever samples are added). The attack is ~17x more sample-efficient than catastrophic forgetting: 115 poisoned samples matched the target-subject damage of 2,000 correct-injection samples while barely touching non-target accuracy. The attack essentially failed on the weaker Qwen3-1.7B-Base, which the authors attribute to its scant encoded medical knowledge (and note such a low-baseline model is unfit for deployment anyway).
core_contribution: The paper introduces a SFT-stage 'rationale poisoning' attack against compact medical LLMs that, unlike trigger-based backdoors, corrupts the model's internal reasoning on a targeted medical subject by injecting a few poisoned multiple-choice samples whose wrong answers are justified by convincing-but-false rationales. Its own contributions are: (1) showing that simple 'knowledge overwriting' (answer/entity swaps without rationales) fails; (2) showing rationale poisoning succeeds with few samples; (3) identifying the critical enabling condition that correct samples of the target subject must be absent ('clean' poison); and (4) showing the attack needs a minimum both in absolute number AND in ratio of poisoned samples, and that it is more efficient and stealthier than degradation caused by catastrophic forgetting from correct-knowledge injection. Note: the near-constant-number backdoor result (250-500 samples) is from Souly et al. (cited related work), used as a contrast point; the paper's own finding is that ratio also matters, unlike in backdoor attacks.
threat_model: An adversary who can contribute a fraction of the supervised fine-tuning dataset used to build a medical LLM (motivated by the low-resource setting where SFT data is scraped from hospitals/exam banks and is 'prone to errors'). The attacker injects a small number of poisoned MedQA-style items on one target subject (e.g., 'fever'), each pairing a wrong answer with a fluent false rationale generated by a strong LLM (GLM-4.6/4.7). Attacker goals are twofold: (a) degrade accuracy on the target subject during inference, and (b) remain stealthy, i.e., keep the poisoned-sample count/ratio low enough to pass dataset quality checks and keep non-target accuracy high enough to pass evaluation. Key attacker constraint discovered: the training set must not contain correct samples of the target subject, and the poison must exceed a minimum number and ratio; otherwise the model's pre-trained medical knowledge and any correct samples overwhelm the poison.
limitations: Scale is small (samples generated 'by thousands' due to token limits); only Qwen3-1.7B and 4B Base tested, no 8B+ due to compute; single dataset (Chinese MedQA) and only two target subjects (fever, inflammation). The authors concede catastrophic-forgetting effects from the required correct samples are inseparable from the poisoning effect. Baselines shift across experiments because runs used different GPU pairs (e.g., Qwen3-4B base overall acc is 0.820 in Tables 1-4 but 0.800 in Table 6), complicating cross-table comparison. The attack is brittle: it fails without a favorable count-and-ratio regime and is neutralized by any correct target-subject data, and it did not transfer to the 1.7B model. No defense is implemented -- only proposed (rationale verification, gradient anomaly detection, adversarial training).

datasets_used: MedQA, simplified-Chinese 5-option branch (train 24,700 / dev 3,425 / test 3,426 QAs)
Target subject 'fever' (发热): 1,591 (6.4%) train, 183 (5.3%) test QAs
quantitative_results: Knowledge overwriting ineffective: fever acc stayed 0.798 (answer), 0.814 (disease), 0.803 (symptom), 0.820 (organ) -- no degradation (Table 1)
Rationale poisoning 125 poison + 1,300 non-fever correct (8.8% ratio), 0 fever-correct: fever acc 0.716 (-8.2%), non-fever 0.789 (-3.2%) (Table 3)
Adding fever-correct samples restores accuracy: fever acc 0.716 (0) -> 0.705 (50) -> 0.727 (100) -> 0.743 (150) -> 0.738 (200) -> 0.754 (400) (Table 3)

Count/ratio sweep (Qwen3-4B): 63+1300 fever 0.776; 125+1300 0.716; 250+1300 0.732; 500+1300 0.661 (non-fever crashes to 0.711); 63+650 0.771 (fails); 125+2600 0.787 (fails, ratio too low); 250+2600 0.727 (succeeds after doubling poison) (Table 4)
Catastrophic forgetting via correct samples by reasoning depth (Qwen3-4B fever acc): base 0.798, shallow 0.792, normal 0.754, deep 0.689; avg char counts 38.6/298.5/1975.8 (Table 2)

Efficiency vs forgetting: 2,000 correct samples dropped overall acc >7% and fever ~4% (0.776->0.732); adding 115 poison samples further cut fever ~4% (to 0.689) -- ~17x fewer samples for equal target damage (Table 6)
Qwen3-1.7B-Base: no poisoning effect, fever acc stayed ~0.656-0.661 vs base 0.656 across 125/250/500 poison (Table 5)

Inflammation replication (Qwen3-4B): base inflammation acc 0.870; 250 (16.1%) poison dropped it 6.4% (to 0.806), non-inflammation -3.5%; more poison needed because base model was strong on inflammation (Table 7)
baselines_compared: Knowledge overwriting via answer poisoning (random option swap) -- ineffective baseline attack
Knowledge overwriting via medical-entity poisoning (o4-mini rewrites disease/symptom/organ) -- ineffective baseline attack
Catastrophic forgetting from correct-knowledge injection (fine-tuning on correct samples only) -- less efficient/stealthy alternative to poisoning
Trigger-based backdoor attacks (Souly et al. near-constant 250-500 samples; Lyu et al. BadCLM) -- cited contrast, not re-run; used to argue ratio matters for rationale poisoning unlike for backdoors
